elements.sent-2411-001: We explore cosmological particle production associated with inflationary fluctuations by comparing gravitational and geometric mechanisms, within a non-minimal Yukawa-like coupling between a inflaton and spacetime curvature.
elements.sent-2411-002: We show under which circumstances the number of geometric particles is comparable to the purely gravitational contribution by introducing the S-matrix formalism and approximating the inflationary potentials through power series.
elements.sent-2411-003: Despite the geometric production is a second-order effect, we show that it cannot be neglected a priori, emphasizing that throughout inflation we do expect geometric particles to form as well as in the reheating phase.
elements.sent-2411-004: This result is investigated in both the Jordan and Einstein frames, selecting inflationary potentials supported by the Planck Satellite outcomes, i.e., the Starobinsky, hilltop, $\alpha$-attractor, and natural inflationary paradigms.
elements.sent-2411-005: We notice that, for large field approaches, the only feasible coupling may occur in case of extremely small field-curvature coupling constants, while the small field frameworks, namely the hilltop and $\alpha$-attractor models with quartic behavior, are essentially ruled out since they do not appear dynamically viable.
elements.sent-2411-006: We then point out that, while dynamically there exists a net equivalence between the Jordan and Einstein frames, the number of particles appears quite different passing from a representation to another one, thus pointing out the existence of a possible quantum frame issue.
elements.sent-2411-007: In summary, while the classical inflationary dynamics is equivalently described in the Jordan and Einstein frames, a viable physical description of particle production valid in both the frames may be hindered by the absence of a complete quantum gravity.
elements.sent-2411-008: The latter could therefore be seen as missing puzzle to address the longstanding challenge of consistently passing between the two frames.
elements.sent-2411-009: Despite inflation remains the simplest paradigm to address the classical Big Bang issues \cite{riotto,Bassett_2006,Vazquez_Gonzalez_2020 , both the inflaton nature and the most viable inflationary potential are nowadays objects of heated debates \cite{starob,higgs,hilltop,natural,universality,Linde_1994 .}}
elements.sent-2411-010: Thus, numerous inflationary models have been proposed throughout the years \cite{Odintsov:2023weg,Bamba:2014daa, Berera:1995ie , within the classes of old and new inflation scenarios, respectively involving phase transitions \cite{guth,linde,newinfl and chaotic graceful exit \cite{LINDE1983177 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-011: Examples are thermodynamic inflation \cite{Andrei,gtd , multi-field inflation \cite{Senatore:2010wk , spinor inflation \cite{Armendariz-Picon:2003wfx,spinor , exotic approaches \cite{mengoni,D_Agostino_2022 and so on, see e.g. \cite{hybrid,vacuuminflation,DVALI199972 .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-012: In a nutshell, single-field inflationary scenarios can be conventionally sorted out into small and large field approaches.
elements.sent-2411-013: In the former case, inflation is caused by a scalar field gradually shifting from small values to larger ones, approaching the minimum of its potential \cite{hilltop,natural,natural1 .}
elements.sent-2411-014: Conversely, for large field models, the process is inverted \cite{starob,1983SvAL....9..302S,ferrara .}
elements.sent-2411-015: In this respect, the most recent Planck satellite findings \cite{planck indicate that the best-fitting inflationary model is the large field Starobinsky potential \cite{starob , turning out to be conformally equivalent to a non-minimally coupled field with Ricci curvature \cite{Kehagias:2013mya .}}}
elements.sent-2411-016: Remarkably, one has not to marvel toward experimental inconsistencies against chaotic potentials, e.g., $V(\phi)=\frac{1 {4}\lambda\phi^4$ appears experimentally disfavored.}
elements.sent-2411-017: However, before discounting this class of models, if a Yukawa-like interacting Lagrangian between curvature and field is included, a significant statistical enhancement occurs, naively suggesting that a possible non-minimal coupling could effectively take place at primordial times.
elements.sent-2411-018: Hence, exploring non-minimally coupled inflationary scenarios becomes essential to pin down the inflaton origin\footnote{Non-minimally coupled inflation gained attention about a decade ago, emerging within the framework of unified theories.}
elements.sent-2411-019: Within this scenario, a perplexing task is understanding how particles can be created across the universe evolution.
elements.sent-2411-020: On the one hand, inflationary fluctuations lead to cosmological inhomogeneities during accelerated expansion.
elements.sent-2411-021: So, by virtue of Einstein's field equations, a pure geometric mechanism of particle production may occur, see Refs.. \cite{friemanpart,cespedes .}
elements.sent-2411-022: On the other hand, field evolution during cosmic expansion produces particles directly from vacuum fluctuations, see Refs, \cite{MUKHANOV1992203,Ford:2021syk,Parker,ema,superheavy,superheavy1 .}
elements.sent-2411-023: This process, dubbed gravitational particle production, is in general related to Bogoljubov transformations for quantum fields.
elements.sent-2411-024: As a matter of fact, particles obtained from gravitational and geometric mechanisms are also associated with entanglement entropy generation \cite{Ball:2005xa,entanglement,Martin-Martinez:2012chf,moradi , whose properties could provide valuable insights into the expansion of the Universe \cite{Pierini:2018wki,Pierini:2015jma,Pierini:2016rkk,Luongo:2019ztg,Capozziello:2013wea,Luongo:2014qoa .}}
elements.sent-2411-025: For example, processes of geometric births of particles may lead to dark matter's origin at early times \cite{Chung:2011ck,Ema:2019yrd,pion,gravitino , either if induced by spectator fields \cite{spect,spectalpha or, afterwards inflation, i.e., in the reheating \cite{Belfiglio:2023eqi,superheavy,superheavy1 .}}}

elements.sent-2411-027: In this paper, based on the above considerations, we compare geometric and gravitational particle productions within non-minimally coupled inflationary scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-028: Thus, the first step is to introduce a Yukawa-like coupling between the inflaton field and scalar curvature for viable inflationary models, i.e., the Starobinsky\footnote{For the sake of completeness, the Starobinsky potential was originally introduced extending gravity through a Lagrangian density $\mathcal L\sim R+\alpha R^2$.}
elements.sent-2411-029: Thus, adding further couplings to the Starobinsky potential may lead to conceptual problems.
elements.sent-2411-030: However, since during the years, several Starobinsky-like potentials have been obtained with different perspectives, it is possible to presume its origins from fields, rather than from extensions of general relativity, see e.g. Refs.. \cite{D_Agostino_2022,mengoni .}
elements.sent-2411-031: For this reason, we do not exclude a priori the Starobinsky model non-minimally coupled with $R$.
elements.sent-2411-032: For each potential, we then investigate whether non-minimal coupling interferes with inflationary dynamics and we definitely find that only the weakly interacting limit on the coupling constant turns out to be physically viable to describe inflation.
elements.sent-2411-033: This suggests that, although small-field models may be partially supported by experimental data, they appear disfavored to explain inflation.
elements.sent-2411-034: To better see this fact, we examine inflationary dynamics in both the Jordan and Einstein frames, assuming a perturbed de Sitter evolution during the slow-roll phase \cite{postma,Capozziello_1997 .}
elements.sent-2411-035: Our intend is thus identifying the physical conditions for each potential allowing for equivalence between the two frames, appearing quite different though.
elements.sent-2411-036: Afterwards, we study the dynamics of scalar inflaton fluctuations from an initial Bunch-Davies vacuum state \cite{Bunch:1978yq at the onset of inflation up to the reheating stage, here formulated as a dust-like epoch\footnote{Including reheating is shown to recover adiabaticity after the strongly non-adiabatic inflationary expansion and, so, the presence of an adiabatic expansion regime is in fact crucial to properly define a vacuum state, and consequently the notion of particle production, immediately after inflation \cite{Birrell:1982ix,Danielsson:2003wb,Brian .. .}}}
elements.sent-2411-037: Accordingly, we explore particle production associated with inflaton fluctuations in the above-depicted scenarios by investigating the Jordan and Einstein frames.
elements.sent-2411-038: Specifically, within a perturbative approach, we introduce a geometric particle production mechanism during inflation.
elements.sent-2411-039: There, as the Bogoljubov coefficients are zero, through a numerical reconstruction of the involved inflationary potentials, we calculate the net pairs and quartets exhibiting different momenta, behaving therefore differently with respect to the gravitational particle production scheme.
elements.sent-2411-040: These particles, moreover, are produced during inflation, instead of being evaluated at the end of its phase and, so, we remarkably show under which circumstances this novel approach to particle production might be taken into consideration significantly contributing alongside the more studied gravitational production.
elements.sent-2411-041: Last but not least, in view of the two particle production treatments, gravitational and geometric, we exploit their consequences to further investigate the frame equivalence problem.
elements.sent-2411-042: In other words, we compute particles in both the two frames and, knowing that the number of particles should be the same in principle, we show that the two frames cannot be fully-equivalent.
elements.sent-2411-043: Motivated by the fact that there exists a dynamical equivalence between the frames, albeit an evident non-equivalence in the number of produced particles, we propose strategies to fix this issue, underling the evident need for having a complete quantum gravity theory that would reconcile the particle production mechanisms in both the Jordan and Einstein frames.
elements.sent-2411-044: The paper is structured as follows: in Sect. \ref{Sect 2 , we introduce the non-minimally coupled inflationary dynamics, highlighting the difference between the Jordan and Einstein frames and showing results about the potentials considered.}

elements.sent-2411-047: Sect. \ref{sect 6 offers a detailed discussion of the main results and their physical implications.}
elements.sent-2411-048: Finally, in Sect. \ref{sect 7 , we conclude with final outlooks and future perspectives.}

elements.section-2411-004: Particle production during and after inflation
elements.sent-2411-197: In the literature, geometric particle production, or more broadly, particle production during inflation are generally ignored as due to the above-discussed issue related to properly identify particle states during the non-adiabatic de Sitter-like expansion.
elements.sent-2411-198: Furthermore, a pure de Sitter spacetime would result in vanishing Bogoljubov coefficients \cite{Birrell:1982ix and, in general, geometric production is seen as a second order expansion with respect to the gravitational particle production.}
elements.sent-2411-199: However, when considering the transition to reheating, gravitational production from the inflaton becomes significant and particle densities can be computed unambiguously.
elements.sent-2411-200: Furthermore, the presence of inhomogenities associated with inflationary fluctuations typically gives rise to additional particles via perturbative processes.
elements.sent-2411-201: In so doing, we discuss below the gravitational and geometric particle productions and distinguish their main features, showing that a possible comparison is instead possible even during inflationary stage.
elements.sent-2411-202: We have defined the cosmological framework describing a single-field inflationary epoch and established coherent boundary conditions.
elements.sent-2411-203: Now, we aim to investigate primordial particle creation processes within a perturbative approach that involves spacetime inhomogeneities \cite{cespedes,friemanpart .}
elements.sent-2411-204: Specifically, we encode the inhomogeneities in the perturbed term of the metric $g_{\mu\nu}$, as defined in Eq. \eqref{perturbed metric .}

elements.sent-2411-335: In this work, we delve into extensions of well-fitting single-field inflationary models to the presence of non-minimal field-curvature coupling \cite{planck .}
elements.sent-2411-336: We characterize the inflationary dynamics by considering different potentials driving the scalar inflaton field, focusing on particle creation processes arising from spacetime expansion and quantum fluctuations and pointing out an underlying quantum non-equivalence between the Jordan and Einstein frames.
elements.sent-2411-337: We here discuss step by step the outcomes of our work.
elements.sent-2411-338: After studying several models, we conclude that the inflationary epoch is consistent with non-minimal coupling, especially for small interaction strengths, both positive and negative.
elements.sent-2411-339: The choice of weak interactions, analogously to particle physics, is necessary for the inflaton to undergo a slow-roll phase, as shown in Sect. \ref{sect 3.1.2 , where we explicitly rule out the conformal coupling prescription.}
elements.sent-2411-340: In addition, strong interactions would lead to a non-physical effective gravitational constant, as shown by Eq. \eqref{geff .}
elements.sent-2411-341: It is worthy to notice that this achievement is general and holds independently of the inflationary potential under consideration.
elements.sent-2411-342: Large field models are preferred over small field ones, providing further confirmation for Starobinsky-like models.
elements.sent-2411-343: In particular, the hilltop and the quartic $\alpha-$attractor models do not present a suitable behavior when coupled with the scalar curvature.
elements.sent-2411-344: Lastly, we highlight that, when working in a weakly interacting regime, the choice between positive and negative coupling does not significantly affect the dynamics; hence, for the sake of brevity, we proceed considering only positive coupling constants.
elements.sent-2411-345: As discussed in the previous section, the main problem associated with a proper evaluation of particle densities in curved backgrounds is represented by the choice of the initial vacuum state for the field.
elements.sent-2411-346: Specifically, for inflationary fluctuations, we adopt the Bunch-Davies vacuum, representing the most used in-vacuum choice currently available \cite{Bunch:1978yq and being a local attractor in the space of initial states for an expanding background.}
elements.sent-2411-347: In addition, we characterize the reheating era to analyze how inflaton fluctuations manage to provide a well-defined out-vacuum state after inflation.
elements.sent-2411-348: We show that the quantized fluctuations satisfy the conditions of adiabatic expansion, Eq. \eqref{adiab , during the reheating stage, so that the cosmological background is complete and consistent to properly address particle production mechanisms.}
elements.sent-2411-349: Due to the infrared divergence, we notice that inflaton fluctuations, and consequently particle densities, increase non-physically in the limit of zero momentum.
elements.sent-2411-350: To address this, we mainly focus on momentum ranges far from the divergence, introducing a proper infrared cutoff which neglects modes outside the Hubble radius at the beginning of inflation.
elements.sent-2411-351: Accordingly, we consider ratios between geometric and gravitational number densities within such ranges.
elements.sent-2411-352: Nevertheless, we clearly highlight that this issue is still present and thus requires further investigations to be refine the vacuum choice at the inflationary onset.
elements.sent-2411-353: In principle, regularization or renormalization techniques should be taken into consideration to heal such divergences.
elements.sent-2411-354: Alternatively, we may need to identify a more appropriate vacuum instead of the conventional Bunch-Davies vacuum.
elements.sent-2411-355: In this scenario, we explore particle production through gravitational and geometric mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-356: To determine the particles number densities, we start from a perturbative approach, approximating the inflationary potentials as series of even powers up to fourth order.
elements.sent-2411-357: We focus on production during the inflationary regime and then we consider the transition to the reheating stage, to properly address the inflaton dynamics when the slow-roll approximation is no longer valid.
elements.sent-2411-358: Further, the total number densities arising from gravitational production has been shown to be zero within a pure de Sitter-like evolution.
elements.sent-2411-359: In computing the total amount of particles produced during inflation and reheating,.
elements.sent-2411-360: Summarizing, we can safely conclude that the geometric particle production significantly contributes to the total amount of particles produced during inflation and reheating.
elements.sent-2411-361: In particular, if dark matter creation can be traced back to such mechanisms, future research should explore both geometric and gravitational scenarios.
elements.subsection-2411-015: Addressing the frame problem through particle production
elements.sent-2411-362: The analysis of the inflationary dynamics involves addressing the frame issue.
elements.sent-2411-363: We recover the inflationary evolution in both the Jordan and Einstein frames, obtaining.
elements.sent-2411-364: However, when investigating particle production in both frames, thus introducing second quantization of the inflaton field, we find that, from such quantum perspective, the two frames leads to different particle amounts.
elements.sent-2411-365: Further, when generalizing this picture to other cosmological eras, we can safely assert that this phenomenon is independent of the background evolution.
elements.sent-2411-366: We underline that, at low energy scales, the difference between the frames is typically negligible, but considering energy scales comparable to those of inflation, such departures are evident when computing particle densities.
elements.sent-2411-367: The disparity in particle production arises from the conformal transformation which defines the Einstein frame and thus makes the Ricci curvature dependent on the scalar field, so that a classical quantity now encodes a quantum field.
elements.sent-2411-368: Consequently, when the system is quantized, part of the information is locked into the Ricci scalar, preventing it from contributing to particle production.
elements.sent-2411-369: This relationship, along with the energy scale dependence, provides further evidence that a full quantum theory of gravity is ultimately required to properly address the issue of frame equivalence.
elements.sent-2411-370: In this paper, we compared two mechanisms of cosmological particle production associated with primordial fluctuations, assuming the scalar inflaton field to be non-minimally coupled to the spacetime curvature through a Yukawa-like interacting Lagrangian.
elements.sent-2411-371: More precisely, we focused on gravitational and geometric particle production and, in particular, we studied under which conditions particles obtained from the geometric term entering the interacting Lagrangian, namely when the Bogoljubov coefficients are set to zero, can significantly contribute to the overall production.
elements.sent-2411-372: In other words, despite the geometric particle production is obtained up to second order of our perturbative approach, it is possible to demand for a net contribution from these particles that cannot be neglected throughout the entire inflationary phase, resulting into a modification of the net energy momentum tensor.
elements.sent-2411-373: To explore this possibility, different classes of inflationary models have been investigated, focusing on small, large and natural inflationary paradigms.
elements.sent-2411-374: To do so, we first analyzed the dynamics of non-minimally coupled inflationary models and concluded that:.
elements.sent-2411-375: In this respect, the hilltop potentials are ruled out, while natural inflation remains dynamically acceptable,.
elements.sent-2411-376: Our dynamical analysis proved that the strength of the non-minimal couplings, across the inflationary regime, might be carefully fine-tuned.
elements.sent-2411-377: Indeed, regardless the potential under investigation, only weak couplings yield consistent slow-roll inflation, ruling out the widely-consolidate conformal coupling.
elements.sent-2411-378: Afterwards, by introducing the unavoidable quantum fluctuations associated with the inflaton field, and the corresponding spacetime inhomogeneities generated by such fluctuations, we determined their dynamics from an initial Bunch-Davies vacuum state up to the reheating phase.
elements.sent-2411-379: In particular, we verified that, before vanishing, the fluctuations reach a state of adiabatic expansion, so that their particle excitations can be properly defined during reheating.
elements.sent-2411-380: Bearing this in mind and, in particular, defining initial and final states allowed us to investigate particle production across the different models we introduced.
elements.sent-2411-381: Specifically, exploring perturbatively the aforementioned gravitational and geometric mechanisms, namely introducing the S-matrix formalism and approximating the inflationary potentials through power series, we observed that the net number of particles produced in the Jordan and Einstein frames differs significantly.
elements.sent-2411-382: Phrasing it differently, while dynamically speaking there exists a net equivalence between the Jordan and Einstein frames, as proved for small coupling constants, the number of particles appears quite different passing from a frame representation to another one, providing a strong frame problem consisting in the discrepancy between the two frames, inevitably caused by the presence of non-minimal coupling.
elements.sent-2411-383: As a possible explanation, the non-equivalence between frames can be attributed to the conformal transformation between the frames themselves, and, in principle, this issue may only be healed within a fully-consistent, but so far missing, quantum gravity puzzle.
elements.sent-2411-384: Further, as widely discussed throughout the paper, we noted that particle production in curved spacetime is generally affected by the issue of vacuum choice and, accordingly, the presence of infrared divergences when starting from a Bunch-Davies vacuum state.
elements.sent-2411-385: In this respect, we proceeded by comparing the geometric and gravitational mechanisms instead of providing total number densities in both frameworks, in the attempt to alleviate this sort of systematic discrepancy.
elements.sent-2411-386: In future works, we aim to better understand the physics related to these fundamental issues by exploring alternative vacuum states.
elements.sent-2411-387: Further, we intend to employ renormalization techniques, to check whether the same issue can be faced using the renormalization group.
elements.sent-2411-388: Additionally, our purpose is to propose a new particle production mechanism involving inhomogeneities beyond the perturbative approach, developing a path integral strategy that could also improve consistency with non-polynomial models.
elements.sent-2411-389: Finally, to give a more complete and comprehensive picture of the inflationary phase, we plan to include possible additional couplings, addressing for example the presence of electromagnetic fields.
elements.sent-2411-390: Indeed, the presence of geometric particles can be associated with the existence of exotic constituents, that may contribute to the net energy-momentum budget of the universe under certain assumptions.
elements.sent-2411-391: This class of particles can be reinterpreted as quasi-particles of geometry, as found in Refs.

elements.sent-2411-411: Then, we show the configuration spaces in both the Jordan and Einstein frames, Fig. \ref{fig dyn .}
elements.sent-2411-412: The attractors around $\phi_0$ and $h_0$ represent the inflationary graceful exit.
elements.sent-2411-413: To avoid redundancy, since we have proved that the inflationary features between negative and positive coupling are similar for small coupling constants, we focus on $\xi >0$.
elements.sent-2411-414: Finally, we compute the tensor-to-scalar ratio and the spectral index to ensure the viability of the non-minimally coupled extensions of the studied potentials with respect to the observational constraints.
elements.sent-2411-415: These parameters are functions of the e-folding number, ranging from $N\simeq70$, namely the onset of the slow-roll phase, to $N\simeq40$ In particular, Fig. \ref{fig rns displays plots reporting the corresponding values of the field.}
